title: The neuroscience of co-regulation and why you need other people
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
Your nervous system wasn't designed to do this alone 

 I spent years believing that healing was a solo sport. That my trauma was mine to carry, mine to f...
htmlContent:
Your nervous system wasn't designed to do this alone

I spent years believing that healing was a solo sport. That my trauma was mine to carry, mine to fix, mine to figure out. I'd sit in my room with my journal and my breathwork apps and my carefully playlists, convinced that if I just tried hard enough, I could regulate myself back to wholeness. And I'd fail. Over and over. Then I'd blame myself for failing. Classic trauma loop, right?

Here's the thing. The science tells a different story. A much more honest one. Your nervous system literally cannot regulate itself in isolation for very long. It wasn't built that way. None of our nervous systems were. We are wired for connection at a biological level that most of us have never been taught to understand.

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, spent decades mapping out how our autonomic nervous system responds to safety and danger. His research shows something that should stop you in your tracks: the ventral vagal pathway, the part of your nervous system associated with calm, connection, and social engagement, only fully activates in the presence of another regulated nervous system. You can't access your deepest states of safety alone. You need another person's nervous system to help you get there. That's not weakness. That's biology.

Think about it. When a baby is distressed, we don't hand them a workbook and tell them to self-regulate. We pick them up. We hold them. We let our calm nervous system speak to their dysregulated one. That's co-regulation. And it doesn't stop being necessary just because you grew up. Your adult nervous system still needs that same basic input, even if you've learned to pretend otherwise.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link). Deb Dana, who works directly with Porges, wrote it as a manual for exactly this kind of work. She calls co-regulation "the biological imperative" and I think she's right. We are not optional to each other. We are necessary.

The myth of the self-sufficient healer

The wellness industry has sold us a lie. A very seductive, very profitable lie. It tells us that if we just buy the right course, do the right protocol, meditate for the right number of minutes, we can fix ourselves entirely on our own. We can become these perfectly regulated islands of calm. No one else required.

That's not how trauma works. And it's not how healing works either.

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, has written extensively about how trauma lives in the body. His work shows that while we can do a lot on our own, the relational piece is non-negotiable. Trauma is often caused by other people, and healing often requires other people too. Not in a therapy session way necessarily, though that helps. In a daily, lived, messy, human way. A friend who lets you cry without fixing you. A partner who doesn't flinch when you dissociate. A community that holds you without demanding you be okay.

If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You (paid link). Mark Wolynn explores how trauma gets passed down through generations and how relational healing can interrupt those patterns. It's a good reminder that your nervous system didn't get this way in a vacuum and it won't heal in one either.

I know how hard this is to hear. Especially if you've been burned by people. Especially if your trust has been shattered. Especially if the people who were supposed to co-regulate with you as a child were the ones who dysregulated you instead. I get it. I've been there. The idea of needing other people can feel like a threat when other people have been the source of your pain.

But here's what I've learned, and what the research keeps showing: the answer isn't to isolate more. The answer is to find safer people, slowly, carefully, at your own pace. To let your nervous system learn, one small interaction at a time, that connection doesn't have to hurt.

What co-regulation actually looks like in practice

Let's get specific. Co-regulation isn't about having deep emotional conversations all the time. It's not about trauma dumping or processing every painful memory with every person you meet. That would be exhausting and frankly, not helpful.

Co-regulation is much simpler and much more ordinary than we think. It's sitting next to someone in comfortable silence. It's having a conversation about nothing important while your nervous systems sync up. It's the feeling of being in a room with someone who is genuinely calm and letting that calm wash over you without having to do anything.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Maybe there was a small release. Maybe a tightening. Both are valid. Your nervous system is already responding to the idea of connection, even through words on a screen. Wild, right?

Janina Fisher, who integrates trauma treatment with attachment theory, talks about how our brains have "implicit relational knowing." We know how to be with others at a level below conscious awareness. Trauma disrupts that knowing, but it doesn't destroy it. The capacity is still there, buried under layers of protection and survival strategies.

So what do you actually do? You start small. You find one person, maybe a therapist, maybe a trusted friend, and you practice being in their presence without having to perform or explain or fix. You let yourself be seen in your dysregulation without immediately trying to make it go away. You notice when your system starts to settle just because someone else is calm near you. That's co-regulation. It's happening all the time, whether you're aware of it or not.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link). Peter Levine's book goes deep into how the body holds trauma and how relational presence is part of releasing it. It's not light reading but it's honest reading.

Why your nervous system is actually trying to protect you

I want to address something directly. If you read this and felt resistance, if your first thought was "I don't need anyone" or "people just hurt me," I see you. I've been you. That response isn't a character flaw. It's a survival strategy that kept you alive when you had no one safe to turn to.

Your nervous system learned, probably very early, that connection was dangerous. That needing people led to disappointment, abandonment, or worse. So it built a fortress. A very effective fortress. And now that fortress is making it hard to let anyone in, even when part of you wants to.

That's not something to fix. It's something to understand and gradually soften. Bessel van der Kolk, whose work on trauma and the brain is foundational, says that "the body keeps the score" of every betrayal, every abandonment, every moment when you needed someone and they weren't there. Your body remembers. And it's doing its job by keeping you alert to potential threat.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). It's not an easy read but it's validating in a way that few books are. Van der Kolk doesn't sugarcoat. He names the damage and also points toward relational healing as a path forward.

Here's what I want you to hold. Your resistance to co-regulation is not a sign that you're broken. It's a sign that your protective system is working exactly as designed. The goal isn't to bulldoze that protection. The goal is to show your nervous system, very slowly, that there are now some people who won't hurt you. That it can begin to trust again, one small interaction at a time.

Sit with that. Let it land. You don't have to do anything with it right now. Just let your system know that this possibility exists, that it's not a threat, that it might even be a source of relief someday.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Anchored (paid link) - A practical guide to working with your nervous system through co-regulation and Polyvagal theory
  • It Didn't Start with You (paid link) - Explores inherited family trauma and how relational healing can interrupt generational patterns
  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) - Bessel van der Kolk's work on how trauma lives in the body and the role of connection in recovery
  • In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) - Peter Levine's exploration of body-based healing and the relational dimension of trauma release

As an Amazon Associate, I earn from qualifying purchases.
